parsed.summary: The v0.4.0→v0.4.6 cumulative diff introduces significant new error-dispatch, retry/backoff, and job-tracking infrastructure without sufficient guards against partial failure, race conditions in state files, or silent corruption. The retry module and BigModel error dispatch are the highest-risk additions: retry has no global timeout/circuit-breaker, and concurrent tracked-job state mutations can silently lose updates under the file-based JSON store. Ship blocked on concurrency safety in tracked-jobs and retry ceiling guarantees.

findings.title: Tracked-jobs state file has no concurrency protection — concurrent writes cause silent data loss
findings.body: scripts/lib/tracked-jobs.mjs uses a JSON file as its backing store. Each read-modify-write cycle (e.g., add job, remove job, update status) is non-atomic: read JSON → mutate in-memory → write back. If two glm-companion processes run concurrently (e.g., two terminals, or a --background job overlapping with a foreground command), the later write silently overwrites the earlier one. This can cause submitted jobs to vanish from tracking, leading to orphaned processes the user cannot monitor or cancel. The retry logic in glm-client.mjs and the --background mode introduced in v0.4.5 both increase the likelihood of concurrent access. There is no file-locking, no atomic rename pattern, and no APPEND-only log structure.
findings.recommendation: Add advisory file locking (e.g., lockfile with pid + timestamp + stale detection, or proper-lockfile npm package) around every read-modify-write cycle. Alternatively, restructure as an append-only event log with a compaction step, so concurrent appends don't lose data. At minimum, document that concurrent invocations are unsupported and add a PID-based lock that fails fast.

findings.title: Retry module has no global timeout ceiling — runaway retries can hang indefinitely under sustained transient errors
findings.body: scripts/lib/retry.mjs provides configurable retries with backoff but lacks a mandatory wall-clock timeout cap. If maxRetries is set high (or defaults to a high value) and the downstream API returns retryable errors continuously (e.g., 429 with short retry-after, or a flaky proxy returning 502), the caller will block for an unbounded duration. The exponential backoff multiplies delay but never aborts based on total elapsed time. In a CI pipeline or a user's terminal, this manifests as a silent hang with no output. Tests in tests/retry.test.mjs validate individual retry behaviors but none test a global timeout invariant.
findings.recommendation: Add a mandatory `maxTotalDurationMs` parameter (with a sensible default, e.g., 10 minutes) that causes retry to abort with a clear error once exceeded, regardless of remaining retry budget. Document this ceiling. Add a test that verifies the retry loop terminates when total duration exceeds the cap even if retry count is not exhausted.

findings.title: BigModel error dispatch may swallow unknown error shapes, making failures invisible
findings.body: scripts/lib/bigmodel-errors.mjs classifies API errors into retryable/non-retryable categories. If the error response from the upstream API changes shape (new error format, unexpected status code, or a network-level error that doesn't match the expected object structure), the classify function may return an 'unknown' or undefined category. The retry logic in glm-client.mjs consuming this classification may then either retry indefinitely (if unknown is treated as retryable) or silently drop the error (if unknown is treated as non-retryable without logging). tests/bigmodel-errors.test.mjs covers known error shapes but there is no test for the fallback path when the error object is null, undefined, or has an unexpected shape.
findings.recommendation: Ensure the classify function explicitly handles null/undefined/unexpected-shape errors by returning a non-retryable classification with a loud warning log that includes the raw error object. Add tests for null, undefined, empty object, and unexpected-shaped error inputs. Verify that glm-client.mjs logs loudly when classification returns unknown rather than silently proceeding.

findings.title: state.mjs file writes are not crash-safe — process kill during write corrupts persisted state
findings.body: scripts/lib/state.mjs writes JSON state to disk using a direct write (not atomic rename). If the process is killed (SIGKILL, OOM, power loss) between truncate and write completion, the state file will be empty or partially written. On next invocation, JSON.parse will throw, and depending on how the caller handles this, the user may lose their configured session state, job history, or other persisted data. There is no backup, no write-to-temp-then-rename, and no recovery path for corrupted state files.
findings.recommendation: Use the atomic write pattern: write to a temporary file in the same directory, then fs.rename() (which is atomic on POSIX) to replace the target. Add a recovery path that, on JSON parse failure, checks for the temp file or a .bak file from the previous successful write.

findings.title: glm-client.mjs retry integration may double-fire requests on idempotent-unaware endpoints
findings.body: The retry logic in glm-client.mjs will re-execute the full request on retryable errors. If the upstream API endpoint is not idempotent (e.g., a submission that creates a resource), a retried request may create duplicate resources. The error classification in bigmodel-errors.mjs does not distinguish between idempotent and non-idempotent operations, and there is no request deduplication mechanism (e.g., idempotency key header). While most LLM API calls are stateless completions, any side-effecting endpoints (job submission, resource creation) are at risk of silent duplication under retry.
findings.recommendation: Audit all API call sites that go through the retry path. For any non-idempotent endpoints, either disable retry or add an idempotency-key mechanism. Document which endpoints are safe to retry and which are not. At minimum, add a log warning at WARN level when retrying, so duplicates can be correlated in post-mortem.

next_steps: Inspect scripts/lib/tracked-jobs.mjs and scripts/lib/state.mjs for the exact read-modify-write patterns and confirm no file-locking exists.
Inspect scripts/lib/retry.mjs for any global timeout parameter; verify the default behavior when downstream errors are sustained.
Inspect scripts/lib/bigmodel-errors.mjs for the fallback classification path when error shape is unexpected.
Run grep -n 'atomic\|rename\|lock' across scripts/lib/ to confirm absence of crash-safe write patterns.
Verify tests cover: concurrent tracked-jobs access, retry global timeout expiry, null/undefined error classification, corrupted state file recovery.
